snapshot.title: Jira CLI — Auth & Cache
snapshot.body:
Phase 1: FileCache + JiraAuth + OAuth login flow + CLI auth commands.

Builds the authentication foundation. After this epic, a user can `jira auth login` via browser, and the token auto-refresh pipeline works transparently.

## Subtasks
- FileCache (file-backed cache with expiry, same .get/.set interface as ExpireValue)
- JiraAuth (RecoverableAuth subclass with renew() using refresh_token)
- Instance discovery (multi-instance resolution from ~/.config/jira-cli/)
- OAuth login flow (browser-based 3LO with PKCE)
- CLI: auth login, auth status, auth logout

## Key design
- TokenStore(ExpireValue()) for access_token (in-memory, ~1h)
- TokenStore(FileCache()) for refresh_token (persisted, rotated by Atlassian)
- Injectable `now` on FileCache for deterministic tests
- See PLAN.md Auth Layer section for full details

snapshot.title: Jira CLI — Schema & Core Client
snapshot.body:
Phase 2: Schema discovery, field resolution, JiraClient + IssueSubClient + SearchSubClient, formatters, and core CLI commands.

After this epic, a user can fetch/search issues, edit them, and do bulk edits — all with friendly field names resolved via the synced schema.

## Subtasks
- Schema sync (fetch fields + createmeta from Jira API, persist to schema.json)
- Field resolution (friendly name → field ID + value expansion, pure function)
- JiraClient + JiraSession + JiraResponse
- IssueSubClient (get, create, edit, delete)
- SearchSubClient (jql, jql_all with auto-pagination)
- Formatters (format_issue, format_issue_list)
- CLI: fields sync/list/schema, issue get/edit, search, bulk edit

## Key design
- resolve_fields() is pure: takes friendly dict + schema, returns Jira API dict
- Schema stored at ~/.config/jira-cli/{cloud_id}/schema.json
- See PLAN.md Schema Discovery and Client Layer sections

snapshot.title: Jira CLI — Templates & Issue Create
snapshot.body:
Phase 3: Template CRUD, default template, merge logic, and issue create command.

After this epic, a user can manage templates and create issues using the full composition: template → --json → --set → resolve_fields().

## Subtasks
- Template CRUD (load, save, list, delete from ~/.config/jira-cli/templates/)
- Default template (set, show, clear via config)
- build_issue_fields() — pure merge + resolve
- CLI: template list/show/create/edit/delete/default
- CLI: issue create with --template, --json, --set, --raw

## Key design
- Shallow override (not deep merge) — predictable, simple
- --raw bypasses all resolution
- See PLAN.md Templates section

snapshot.title: FileCache: file-backed cache with expiry
snapshot.body:
Implement FileCache in src/jira/cache.py — a file-backed key-value store with per-key expiry. Same .get(key)/.set(key, value, seconds) interface as ExpireValue and django_cache, so TokenStore can use it as a backend.

## What to do (TDD)

### Tests first (tests/test_cache.py)
Write tests for FileCache:
- get() returns default when file doesn't exist
- get() returns default when key doesn't exist
- get() returns default when key is expired
- get() returns value when valid (not expired)
- get() returns value when no expiry (seconds_to_expire=None means never expires)
- set() creates parent directories
- set() writes value and expiry timestamp
- set() overwrites existing key, preserves other keys
- Injectable `now` for deterministic expiry tests (use time_machine or inject callable)

### Implementation (src/jira/cache.py)
```python
class FileCache:
    def __init__(self, path, now=utc_now):
        self.path = Path(path).expanduser()
        self._now = now

    def get(self, key, default=None): ...
    def set(self, key, value, seconds_to_expire): ...
```

Storage format (JSON file):
```json
{"key": {"value": "...", "expires_at": "2026-03-24T20:00:00+00:00"}}
```

## Key conventions
- Injectable `now` as default arg (not reaching for global)
- No underscore prefixes
- Let Pydantic handle coercion if models are added later
- Use tmp_path fixture for file tests

## Done when
- All tests pass
- `uv run ruff check src/ tests/` passes
- FileCache works with TokenStore (same interface as ExpireValue)

## Files
- src/jira/cache.py (new)
- tests/test_cache.py (new)

## Reference
- See PLAN.md 'FileCache' section for full implementation sketch

snapshot.title: JiraAuth: RecoverableAuth with refresh token renewal
snapshot.body:
Implement JiraAuth in src/jira/auth.py — a RecoverableAuth subclass that auto-refreshes access tokens using a persisted refresh token. Same pattern as ExistingAuth.

## What to do (TDD)

### Tests first (tests/test_auth.py)
Write tests for JiraAuth:
- renew() POSTs refresh_token to TOKEN_URL with correct payload
- renew() returns (access_token, expires_in) from response
- renew() saves rotated refresh_token when response includes one
- renew() preserves existing refresh_token when response doesn't include one
- renew() raises JiraAuthError when no refresh_token is stored
- Token property returns cached value when still valid (via ExpireValue)
- Token property calls renew() when expired

Mock the HTTP POST in renew() tests (external API boundary — this is an acceptable mock per conventions). Use a real ExpireValue + FileCache (with tmp_path) for the token stores.

### Implementation (src/jira/auth.py)
```python
class JiraAuthError(Exception): ...

class JiraAuth(RecoverableAuth):
    AUTH_URL = "https://auth.atlassian.com/authorize"
    TOKEN_URL = "https://auth.atlassian.com/oauth/token"
    SESSION_CLASS = ProSession

    def __init__(self, token: TokenStore, client_id: str, refresh: TokenStore):
        self.client_id = client_id
        self.refresh = refresh
        super().__init__(token)

    def renew(self) -> tuple[TokenData, TokenExpiry]: ...
```

## Dependencies
- Requires FileCache (bean-cd0a1792) for the refresh token store
- Requires requestspro: RecoverableAuth, TokenStore, ExpireValue, ProSession

## Key conventions
- Inject dependencies (token store, refresh store), don't construct inside
- Constants as class attributes
- No underscore prefixes

## Done when
- All tests pass
- `uv run ruff check src/ tests/` passes
- JiraAuth.renew() works with real TokenStore(ExpireValue) + TokenStore(FileCache)

## Files

- tests/test_auth.py (new)

snapshot.title: Instance discovery: multi-instance resolution
snapshot.body:
Implement instance discovery in src/jira/config.py — resolves which Jira instance to use from CLI flag, env var, config default, or single-instance auto-detect.

## What to do (TDD)

### Tests first (tests/test_config.py)
Write tests for discover_instance_dir():
- Returns instance dir when --instance matches site name

- Auto-detects when only one instance exists
- Raises error when multiple instances and no selection

### Implementation (src/jira/config.py)
```python
BASE_DIR = "~/.config/jira-cli"

def discover_instance_dir(instance=None, base_dir=BASE_DIR) -> Path:
    """Resolve instance directory.

    Resolution order:
    1. instance arg (matches site name in config.json)
    2. JIRA_INSTANCE env var
    3. config.json -> 'default'
    4. Only one instance exists? Use it.
    5. Error
    """
```

Also implement:

- Per-instance config read (instance_dir/config.json with cloud_id, client_id, site)

## Key conventions
- base_dir as default argument, not hardcoded in body
- Use FileCache for config.json access (or plain json read/write — simpler if no expiry needed)
- Pure functions where possible, I/O isolated

snapshot.title: OAuth login flow: browser-based 3LO with PKCE
snapshot.body:
Implement the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow with PKCE in src/jira/auth.py — the one-time browser login that gets the initial access_token + refresh_token.

## What to do

### Implementation (add to src/jira/auth.py)
Add a `login()` function (or method) that:
1. Generates PKCE code_verifier + code_challenge
2. Opens browser to Atlassian's authorize URL with scopes, redirect_uri (localhost callback)
3. Starts a temporary local HTTP server to receive the callback
4. Exchanges the authorization code for access_token + refresh_token
5. Fetches accessible resources to get cloud_id + site name
6. Saves client_id, cloud_id, site to instance config.json
7. Saves refresh_token via FileCache
8. Sets this instance as default (first login)

### Scopes needed
- read:jira-work, write:jira-work, read:jira-user, manage:jira-project, offline_access

### Tests (tests/test_auth.py)
Testing the full browser flow is hard (interactive). Focus on:
- PKCE code_verifier/challenge generation (pure function, testable)
- Token exchange: mock the POST, verify correct payload
- Accessible resources parsing: mock GET, verify cloud_id extraction
- Config saving: verify files written to correct paths (use tmp_path)

## Dependencies
- Requires FileCache (bean-cd0a1792)
- Requires instance discovery (bean-1d58d17c)

## Done when
- PKCE generation tests pass
- Token exchange tests pass
- Config persistence tests pass
- Manual test: `uv run jira auth login` opens browser and completes flow (test this yourself)

## Files

- tests/test_auth.py (extend)

## Reference

snapshot.title: CLI: auth login, auth status, auth logout
snapshot.body:
Implement the auth subcommands in src/jira/cli.py using argparse. These are the first CLI commands — set up the parse()/cli() pattern here.

## What to do (TDD)

### Tests first (tests/test_cli.py)
Test the parse() function:
- parse(['auth', 'login']) → command='auth', subcommand='login'
- parse(['auth', 'status']) → command='auth', subcommand='status'
- parse(['auth', 'logout']) → command='auth', subcommand='logout'
- parse(['--instance', 'acme', 'auth', 'login']) → instance='acme'
- parse([]) → prints help (or error)

### Implementation (src/jira/cli.py)
```python
def parse(argv=None) -> argparse.Namespace:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(prog='jira')
    parser.add_argument('--instance', help='Jira instance (site name)')
    subparsers = parser.add_subparsers(dest='command')

    # auth subcommands
    auth_parser = subparsers.add_parser('auth')
    auth_sub = auth_parser.add_subparsers(dest='subcommand')
    auth_sub.add_parser('login')
    auth_sub.add_parser('status')
    auth_sub.add_parser('logout')
    return parser.parse_args(argv)

def cli(argv=None):
    args = parse(argv)
    # dispatch to auth handlers
```

### CLI behavior
- `auth login` — calls login() flow, prints success + site name
- `auth status` — shows: logged in/out, site, cloud_id, token valid/expired
- `auth logout` — removes stored tokens, prints confirmation

### Also implement src/jira/__main__.py
```python
from jira.cli import cli
cli()
```

## Dependencies
- Requires JiraAuth (bean-9b922e7d)
- Requires OAuth login flow (bean-35c47b66)
- Requires instance discovery (bean-1d58d17c)

## Key conventions
- parse() is pure (returns Namespace, no I/O)
- cli() is the I/O boundary
- JSON output to stdout
- No business logic in cli.py

## Done when
- parse() tests pass
- `uv run jira auth status` works (shows 'not logged in' if no tokens)
- `uv run ruff check src/ tests/` passes

snapshot.title: Field resolution: friendly names to Jira API format
snapshot.body:
Implement resolve_fields() in src/jira/schema.py — a pure function that does name resolution (friendly → field ID) and value expansion (flat value → nested dict per field type).

## What to do (TDD)

### Tests first (tests/test_schema.py — extend)
Test resolve_fields() with a fixture schema dict:
- String field: 'summary': 'Fix bug' → 'summary': 'Fix bug' (passthrough)
- Number field: 'story_points': 5 → 'customfield_10036': 5
- Issue link: 'parent': 'DEV-22173' → 'parent': {'key': 'DEV-22173'}
- System option: 'priority': 'P1: High' → 'priority': {'name': 'P1: High'}
- Custom option: 'team': 'Payments' → 'customfield_10001': {'value': 'Payments'}
- Array of named: 'components': ['Pod: Payments'] → 'components': [{'name': 'Pod: Payments'}]
- Array of strings: 'labels': ['urgent'] → 'labels': ['urgent']
- Unknown field type: passes through unchanged
- Already-structured value (dict/list of dicts): passes through (no double-wrapping)
- Multiple fields in one call

### Implementation (extend src/jira/schema.py)
```python
def resolve_fields(friendly: dict, schema: dict) -> dict:
    """Translate friendly names → Jira field IDs AND expand values to API format."""
```

Value expansion rules are driven by the schema's field type:
- string → passthrough
- number → passthrough
- issuelink → {'key': value}
- option (system: priority, status) → {'name': value}
- option (custom) → {'value': value}
- array of named → [{'name': v} for v in value]
- array of strings → passthrough

## Dependencies
- Requires schema sync (bean-ef3d29e5) — needs the schema structure defined

## Key conventions
- This is a PURE function — no I/O, no side effects
- Test with hardcoded schema fixture, not by reading files
- One assertion purpose per test

snapshot.title: JiraClient + IssueSubClient + SearchSubClient
snapshot.body:
Implement the core client layer in src/jira/client.py — JiraSession, JiraResponse, JiraClient (MainClient), IssueSubClient, and SearchSubClient.

## What to do (TDD)

### Tests first (tests/test_client.py)
Create a make_session() helper that returns a mock JiraSession (acceptable mock — external HTTP boundary).

IssueSubClient tests:
- get() calls GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key} with correct fields param
- create() calls POST /rest/api/3/issue with correct payload
- edit() calls PUT /rest/api/3/issue/{key} with correct payload
- delete() calls DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}

SearchSubClient tests:
- jql() calls POST /rest/api/3/search with query and fields
- jql_all() paginates (mock multiple pages, verify all collected)

JiraClient tests:
- from_config() wires up auth, session, sub-clients correctly (use tmp_path for config)

### Implementation (src/jira/client.py)
```python
class JiraSession(ProSession):
    RESPONSE_CLASS = JiraResponse

class JiraResponse(ProResponse):
    """Normalizes Jira API responses — pagination, error extraction."""

class JiraClient(MainClient):
    def __init__(self, session):
        super().__init__(session, audit=False)
        self.issue = IssueSubClient(session)
        self.search = SearchSubClient(session)

    @classmethod
    def from_config(cls, instance=None): ...

class IssueSubClient(Client):
    def get(self, issue_key, fields=None, expand=None) -> dict
    def create(self, project, issue_type, summary, **fields) -> dict
    def edit(self, issue_key, **fields) -> dict
    def delete(self, issue_key) -> None

class SearchSubClient(Client):
    def jql(self, query, fields=None, max_results=50) -> list[dict]
    def jql_all(self, query, fields=None) -> list[dict]
```

## Dependencies
- Requires JiraAuth (bean-9b922e7d)
- Requires instance discovery (bean-1d58d17c)
- Requires requestspro: MainClient, Client, ProSession, ProResponse

## Key conventions
- Sub-clients take session as injected dependency
- from_config() is the only place that constructs the full stack
- No business logic in client — just HTTP wiring

## Done when
- All tests pass
- `uv run ruff check src/ tests/` passes

## Files
- src/jira/client.py (new)
- tests/test_client.py (new)

## Reference
- See PLAN.md 'Client Layer' section

snapshot.title: build_issue_fields: template merge + field resolution
snapshot.body:
Implement build_issue_fields() in src/jira/templates.py — the pure merge function that composes template → --json → --set → resolve_fields().

## What to do (TDD)

### Tests first (tests/test_templates.py — extend)
Test build_issue_fields() with a fixture schema:
- Template only: returns resolved template fields
- Template + json override: json keys override template keys (shallow)
- Template + json + cli flags: cli flags win over json and template
- No template: works with just json and/or flags
- Shallow override: array field in json fully replaces template array (not appended)
- Empty inputs: returns empty dict
- Integration: full pipeline with real-ish schema, verifying field IDs + value expansion

### Implementation (extend src/jira/templates.py)
```python
def build_issue_fields(
    template: dict | None,
    json_override: dict | None,
    cli_flags: dict,
    schema: dict,
) -> dict:
    """Pure merge: template | json | flags. Then resolve friendly names + expand values."""
    base = template or {}
    merged = {**base, **(json_override or {}), **cli_flags}
    return resolve_fields(merged, schema)
```

## Dependencies
- Requires field resolution (bean-ec7a488f) — resolve_fields() must exist
- Requires template CRUD (bean-e1d10db5) — load_template() for loading

## Key conventions
- PURE function — takes data in, returns data out
- Shallow override only (not deep merge) — see PLAN.md for rationale
- Test with hardcoded fixtures, not by reading files

snapshot.title: FileCache: file-backed cache with expiry
snapshot.body:
Implement FileCache in src/jira/cache.py — a file-backed key-value store with per-key expiry. Same .get(key)/.set(key, value, seconds) interface as ExpireValue and django_cache, so TokenStore can use it as a backend.

## Interface

```python
class FileCache:
    def __init__(self, path, now=utc_now):
        self.path = Path(path).expanduser()
        self._now = now

    def get(self, key, default=None): ...
    def set(self, key, value, seconds_to_expire): ...
```

Storage format (JSON file):
```json
{"key": {"value": "...", "expires_at": "2026-03-24T20:00:00+00:00"}}
```

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

Work through these one at a time — write one test, make it pass, move to the next:

1. set() writes a value to a JSON file (creates parent dirs if needed)
2. get() reads it back
3. get() returns default when file doesn't exist
4. get() returns default when key doesn't exist
5. set() with expiry — get() returns value when not yet expired
6. get() returns default when expired
7. set() with no expiry (None) — value never expires
8. set() overwrites existing key, preserves other keys

Start with the tracer bullet: set + get round-trip (behaviors 1-2). That proves the path works.

## Key conventions
- Injectable `now` as default arg (not reaching for global) — use it for deterministic expiry tests
- Use tmp_path fixture for file tests
- No underscore prefixes
- No mocks — test with real files via tmp_path

snapshot.title: build_issue_fields: template merge + field resolution
snapshot.body:
Implement build_issue_fields() in src/jira/templates.py — the pure merge function that composes template → --json → --set → resolve_fields().

## Interface

```python
def build_issue_fields(
    template: dict | None,
    json_override: dict | None,
    cli_flags: dict,
    schema: dict,
) -> dict:
    """Pure merge: template | json | flags. Then resolve friendly names + expand values."""
    base = template or {}
    merged = {**base, **(json_override or {}), **cli_flags}
    return resolve_fields(merged, schema)
```

snapshot.title: FileCache: file-backed cache with expiry
snapshot.body:
Implement FileCache in src/jira/cache.py — a file-backed key-value store with per-key expiry. Same .get(key)/.set(key, value, seconds) interface as ExpireValue and django_cache, so TokenStore can use it as a backend.

## Interface

```python
class FileCache:
    def __init__(self, path, now=utc_now):
        self.path = Path(path).expanduser()
        self._now = now

    def get(self, key, default=None): ...
    def set(self, key, value, seconds_to_expire): ...
```

Storage format (JSON file):
```json
{"key": {"value": "...", "expires_at": "2026-03-24T20:00:00+00:00"}}
```

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

Work through these one at a time — write one test, make it pass, move to the next:

1. set() writes a value to a JSON file (creates parent dirs if needed)
2. get() reads it back
3. get() returns default when file doesn't exist
4. get() returns default when key doesn't exist
5. set() with expiry — get() returns value when not yet expired
6. get() returns default when expired
7. set() with no expiry (None) — value never expires
8. set() overwrites existing key, preserves other keys

Start with the tracer bullet: set + get round-trip (behaviors 1-2). That proves the path works.

## Key conventions
- Injectable `now` as default arg (not reaching for global) — use it for deterministic expiry tests
- Use tmp_path fixture for file tests
- No underscore prefixes
- No mocks — test with real files via tmp_path

## Done when
- All behaviors covered with tests
- `uv run ruff check src/ tests/` passes
- FileCache works with TokenStore (same interface as ExpireValue)

## Files
- src/jira/cache.py (new)
- tests/test_cache.py (new)

## Reference
- See PLAN.md 'FileCache' section for full implementation sketch

snapshot.title: Instance discovery: multi-instance resolution
snapshot.body:
Implement instance discovery in src/jira/config.py — resolves which Jira instance to use from CLI flag, env var, config default, or single-instance auto-detect.

## Interface

```python
BASE_DIR = "~/.config/jira-cli"

def discover_instance_dir(instance=None, base_dir=BASE_DIR) -> Path:
    """Resolve instance directory.

    Resolution order:
    1. instance arg (matches site name in config.json)
    2. JIRA_INSTANCE env var
    3. config.json -> 'default'
    4. Only one instance exists? Use it.
    5. Error
    """
```

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

1. Auto-detects when only one instance dir exists (simplest happy path — tracer bullet)
2. Returns instance dir when `instance` arg matches a site name in that dir's config.json
3. Returns instance dir from JIRA_INSTANCE env var
4. Returns default instance from base config.json
5. Raises error when multiple instances and no selection
6. Raises error when instance name doesn't match any site

Start with behavior 1 — create one fake instance dir in tmp_path, verify it's found.

## Key conventions
- base_dir as default argument, not hardcoded in body
- Pure functions where possible, I/O isolated
- Use tmp_path to create fake config directory structures

## Done when
- All behaviors covered with tests
- `uv run ruff check src/ tests/` passes

## Files
- src/jira/config.py (new)
- tests/test_config.py (new)

## Reference
- See PLAN.md 'Instance Resolution' section

snapshot.title: JiraAuth: RecoverableAuth with refresh token renewal
snapshot.body:
Implement JiraAuth in src/jira/auth.py — a RecoverableAuth subclass that auto-refreshes access tokens using a persisted refresh token. Same pattern as ExistingAuth.

## Interface

```python
class JiraAuthError(Exception): ...

class JiraAuth(RecoverableAuth):
    AUTH_URL = "https://auth.atlassian.com/authorize"
    TOKEN_URL = "https://auth.atlassian.com/oauth/token"
    SESSION_CLASS = ProSession

    def __init__(self, token: TokenStore, client_id: str, refresh: TokenStore):
        self.client_id = client_id
        self.refresh = refresh
        super().__init__(token)

    def renew(self) -> tuple[TokenData, TokenExpiry]: ...
```

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

1. renew() raises JiraAuthError when no refresh_token is stored
2. renew() POSTs refresh_token to TOKEN_URL and returns (access_token, expires_in)
3. renew() saves rotated refresh_token when Atlassian returns a new one
4. renew() preserves existing refresh_token when response doesn't rotate it
5. Token property returns cached value when still valid (via ExpireValue)
6. Token property triggers renew() when expired

Start with behavior 1 (error path) — simplest slice, proves the class instantiates correctly.

Mock the HTTP POST in renew() tests — this is an external API boundary (acceptable mock per conventions). Use real ExpireValue + FileCache (with tmp_path) for the token stores.

## Dependencies
- Requires FileCache (bean-cd0a1792) for the refresh token store
- Requires requestspro: RecoverableAuth, TokenStore, ExpireValue, ProSession

## Key conventions
- Inject dependencies (token store, refresh store), don't construct inside
- Constants as class attributes
- No underscore prefixes

## Done when
- All behaviors covered with tests
- `uv run ruff check src/ tests/` passes

## Files
- src/jira/auth.py (new)
- tests/test_auth.py (new)

- See PLAN.md 'Auth Class' and 'How it flows' sections

snapshot.title: OAuth login flow: browser-based 3LO with PKCE
snapshot.body:
Implement the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow with PKCE in src/jira/auth.py — the one-time browser login that gets the initial access_token + refresh_token.

## Interface

Add a login() function that runs the full OAuth flow:
1. Generates PKCE code_verifier + code_challenge
2. Opens browser to Atlassian authorize URL
3. Starts temporary local HTTP server for callback
4. Exchanges authorization code for tokens
5. Fetches accessible resources to get cloud_id + site name
6. Saves config + refresh_token to instance dir
7. Sets instance as default on first login

Scopes: read:jira-work, write:jira-work, read:jira-user, manage:jira-project, offline_access

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

The full browser flow is interactive, so focus on the testable pure/mockable parts:

1. PKCE code_verifier generation produces valid base64url string of correct length
2. PKCE code_challenge is correct SHA256 of verifier
3. Token exchange sends correct POST payload (mock the HTTP call)
4. Accessible resources response is parsed to extract cloud_id + site
5. Config files are written to correct paths after successful login (use tmp_path)

Start with PKCE generation — pure function, no I/O, simplest slice.

## Dependencies
- Requires FileCache (bean-cd0a1792)
- Requires instance discovery (bean-1d58d17c)

## Done when
- Testable parts covered with tests
- Manual test: `uv run jira auth login` opens browser and completes flow
- `uv run ruff check src/ tests/` passes

## Files
- src/jira/auth.py (extend)
- tests/test_auth.py (extend)

## Reference
- See PLAN.md 'Initial Login' section
- Same flow proven in atlassian-mcp-oauth.py

snapshot.title: Formatters: pure response transformers
snapshot.body:
Implement formatters in src/jira/formatters.py — pure functions that transform raw Jira API responses into clean, agent-friendly JSON shapes.

## Interface

```python
def format_issue(raw: dict) -> dict:
    """Raw Jira issue → clean {key, summary, status, assignee, ...}"""

def format_issue_list(raw_issues: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    """List of raw issues → list of clean dicts"""

def format_sprint(raw: dict) -> dict:
    """Raw sprint → {id, name, state, startDate, endDate}"""

def format_transition(raw: dict) -> dict:
    """Raw transition → {id, name, to_status}"""
```

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

1. format_issue() extracts key and summary from nested raw response (tracer bullet — proves the shape)
2. format_issue() extracts status, assignee, priority, issuetype
3. format_issue() handles null/missing assignee gracefully (returns None, not crash)
4. format_issue_list() transforms a list of raw issues
5. format_sprint() extracts id, name, state, dates from raw sprint
6. format_transition() extracts id, name, to_status from raw transition

Start with behavior 1 — minimal raw fixture, prove one field extraction works.

## Key conventions
- ALL PURE — no I/O, no imports from client/store
- Each function takes a raw dict, returns a clean dict
- Readability over DRY in tests — each test has its own raw fixture inline

snapshot.title: Schema sync: fetch and persist Jira instance field config
snapshot.body:
Implement schema sync in src/jira/schema.py — fetches all fields and per-project per-type metadata from the Jira API and persists to schema.json.

## Interface

```python
def friendly_name(name: str) -> str:
    """'Story Points' → 'story_points'"""

def build_field_registry(raw_fields: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """GET /rest/api/3/field response → {friendly_name: {id, type, name?, system?}}"""

def build_type_schema(raw_createmeta: dict) -> dict:
    """Per-type createmeta → {required: [...], fields: {name: {type, required, allowed?}}}"""

def sync(client, instance_dir, project=None):
    """Fetch from API and write schema.json. I/O boundary."""
```

API endpoints:
- GET /rest/api/3/field → all fields
- GET /rest/api/3/issue/createmeta/{project}/issuetypes → issue types
- GET /rest/api/3/issue/createmeta/{project}/issuetypes/{typeId} → per-type fields

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

1. friendly_name() converts 'Story Points' → 'story_points' (tracer bullet — pure, no setup)
2. friendly_name() handles edge cases: already lowercase, single word, extra spaces
3. build_field_registry() maps a raw field list to {friendly_name: {id, type, ...}} — use a small hardcoded fixture (2-3 fields)
4. build_field_registry() distinguishes system fields (summary, status) from custom fields
5. build_type_schema() extracts required fields and allowed values from createmeta response
6. sync() writes schema.json to correct path with synced_at timestamp (test with tmp_path, mock the client HTTP calls)

Start with friendly_name() — pure function, zero dependencies.

snapshot.title: JiraClient + IssueSubClient + SearchSubClient
snapshot.body:
Implement the core client layer in src/jira/client.py — JiraSession, JiraResponse, JiraClient (MainClient), IssueSubClient, and SearchSubClient.

## Interface

```python
class JiraSession(ProSession):
    RESPONSE_CLASS = JiraResponse

class JiraResponse(ProResponse):
    """Normalizes Jira API responses — pagination, error extraction."""

class JiraClient(MainClient):
    def __init__(self, session): ...
    @classmethod
    def from_config(cls, instance=None): ...

class IssueSubClient(Client):
    def get(self, issue_key, fields=None, expand=None) -> dict
    def create(self, project, issue_type, summary, **fields) -> dict
    def edit(self, issue_key, **fields) -> dict
    def delete(self, issue_key) -> None

class SearchSubClient(Client):
    def jql(self, query, fields=None, max_results=50) -> list[dict]
    def jql_all(self, query, fields=None) -> list[dict]
```

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

Use a make_session() helper that returns a mock JiraSession (acceptable mock — external HTTP boundary).

1. IssueSubClient.get() calls GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key} (tracer bullet — proves sub-client wiring)
2. IssueSubClient.get() passes fields param correctly
3. IssueSubClient.create() POSTs to /rest/api/3/issue with correct payload
4. IssueSubClient.edit() PUTs to /rest/api/3/issue/{key}
5. IssueSubClient.delete() sends DELETE to /rest/api/3/issue/{key}
6. SearchSubClient.jql() POSTs to /rest/api/3/search with query and fields
7. SearchSubClient.jql_all() paginates across multiple pages
8. JiraClient.from_config() wires auth + session + sub-clients from config files (use tmp_path)

Start with behavior 1 — proves the sub-client pattern works end-to-end.

## Dependencies
- Requires JiraAuth (bean-9b922e7d)
- Requires instance discovery (bean-1d58d17c)
- Requires requestspro: MainClient, Client, ProSession, ProResponse

## Key conventions
- Sub-clients take session as injected dependency
- from_config() is the only place that constructs the full stack
- No business logic in client — just HTTP wiring

snapshot.body:
Implement the auth subcommands in src/jira/cli.py using argparse. These are the first CLI commands — establish the parse()/cli() pattern here.

## Interface

```python
def parse(argv=None) -> argparse.Namespace:
    """Pure parsing. Returns a Namespace. No I/O."""

def cli(argv=None):
    """Entry point. Parses, dispatches, handles I/O."""
```

Commands:
- `jira auth login` — runs OAuth browser flow, prints success + site name
- `jira auth status` — shows logged in/out, site, cloud_id, token state
- `jira auth logout` — removes stored tokens, prints confirmation

2. parse(['auth', 'status']) and parse(['auth', 'logout']) parse correctly
3. parse(['--instance', 'acme', 'auth', 'login']) captures instance flag
4. parse([]) shows help or errors gracefully

Test parse() directly — it's pure, no CliRunner needed. The cli() dispatch to auth handlers is wiring (thin layer, tested manually or via integration).

- Requires JiraAuth (bean-9b922e7d)
- Requires OAuth login flow (bean-35c47b66)
- Requires instance discovery (bean-1d58d17c)

- parse() is pure (returns Namespace, no I/O)
- cli() is the I/O boundary — no business logic

snapshot.title: Jira CLI — Auth & Cache
snapshot.body:
Phase 1: FileCache + JiraAuth + OAuth login flow + CLI auth commands.

Builds the authentication foundation. After this epic, a user can `jira auth login` via browser, and the token auto-refresh pipeline works transparently.

## Subtasks
- FileCache (file-backed cache with expiry, same .get/.set interface as ExpireValue)
- JiraAuth (RecoverableAuth subclass with renew() using refresh_token)
- Instance discovery (multi-instance resolution from ~/.config/jira-cli/)
- OAuth login flow (browser-based 3LO with PKCE)
- CLI: auth login, auth status, auth logout

## Key design
- TokenStore(ExpireValue()) for access_token (in-memory, ~1h)
- TokenStore(FileCache()) for refresh_token (persisted, rotated by Atlassian)
- Injectable `now` on FileCache for deterministic tests
- See PLAN.md Auth Layer section for full details

snapshot.title: build_issue_fields: template merge + field resolution
snapshot.body:
Implement build_issue_fields() in src/jira/templates.py — the pure merge function that composes template → --json → --set → resolve_fields().

## Interface

```python
def build_issue_fields(
    template: dict | None,
    json_override: dict | None,
    cli_flags: dict,
    schema: dict,
) -> dict:
    """Pure merge: template | json | flags. Then resolve friendly names + expand values."""
    base = template or {}
    merged = {**base, **(json_override or {}), **cli_flags}
    return resolve_fields(merged, schema)
```

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

Use a small hardcoded schema fixture:

1. Template only: resolved fields come through (tracer bullet)
2. JSON override: json keys replace template keys
3. CLI flags win: flags override both json and template
4. Shallow override: array in json fully replaces template array (NOT appended)
5. No template: works with just json and/or flags
6. All empty inputs: returns empty dict
7. Full pipeline: template + json + flags with real-ish schema, verify field IDs + value expansion

Start with behavior 1 — template dict in, resolved dict out.

## Dependencies
- Requires field resolution (bean-ec7a488f) — resolve_fields() must exist
- Requires template CRUD (bean-e1d10db5) — load_template() for loading

## Key conventions
- PURE function — takes data in, returns data out
- Shallow override only (not deep merge) — see PLAN.md for rationale
- Test with hardcoded fixtures, not by reading files

## Done when
- All merge behaviors covered
- `uv run ruff check src/ tests/` passes

snapshot.title: Shell completion with argcomplete
snapshot.body:
Add shell completion for bash/zsh/fish using argcomplete. Completes commands, subcommands, template names, field names, and enum values from schema.

## Interface

```bash
jira completion install    # set up shell hook (bash/zsh/fish)
jira completion bash       # output bash completion script
jira completion zsh        # output zsh completion script
```

Completion targets:
- Commands and subcommands (static, from argparse)
- `--template <TAB>` → template names from instance templates dir
- `--set <TAB>` → field names from schema (e.g. story_points=, team=, priority=)
- `--set priority=<TAB>` → enum values from schema (e.g. P0: Critical, P1: High)
- `--set components=<TAB>` → allowed values from schema
- Issue keys: not live-completed (too slow), but could cache recent keys

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

1. Add argcomplete dependency and activate on the parser (tracer bullet — commands/subcommands complete)
2. Custom completer for `--template`: reads template names from instance templates dir
3. Custom completer for `--set` field names: reads field keys from schema.json
4. Custom completer for `--set` enum values: splits on `=`, looks up allowed values in schema
5. `jira completion install` command that outputs shell-specific setup instructions
6. Test completers as pure functions (they take a prefix, return matches)

Start with behavior 1 — just `argcomplete.autocomplete(parser)` and verify tab completion works for commands.

## Key design
- Completers are pure functions: take prefix + schema/config, return list of strings
- Schema is read once from disk (no API calls during completion — must be fast)
- `--set` completer splits current word on `=`: left side completes field names, right side completes enum values
- argcomplete handles the shell integration plumbing

## Dependencies
- Requires schema sync (schema.json must exist for field/enum completion)
- Requires templates (templates dir must exist for template completion)

## Done when
- `jira <TAB>` completes commands
- `--template <TAB>` completes template names
- `--set <TAB>` completes field names
- `--set priority=<TAB>` completes enum values
- `uv run pytest` passes
- `uv run ruff check src/ tests/` passes

## Files
- src/jira/cli.py (extend — add argcomplete, completion subcommand)
- src/jira/completers.py (new — pure completer functions)
- tests/test_completers.py (new)
- pyproject.toml (add argcomplete dependency)

snapshot.title: Switch to public OAuth client with hardcoded client_id
snapshot.body:
Remove client_secret from the auth flow and hardcode the client_id so users just run `jira auth login` with no prompts.

## What needs to change

1. Hardcode CLIENT_ID in auth.py (from the Atlassian app configured as public client)
2. Remove client_secret from: exchange_code(), JiraAuth.renew(), save_login_config(), login()
3. Remove client_secret prompt from cli.py auth login handler
4. Remove client_secret from config.json persistence and auth status display
5. Update tests that pass client_secret

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

1. login() uses hardcoded client_id, no secret in token exchange (tracer bullet)
2. renew() sends no client_secret in refresh payload
3. CLI auth login has no prompts — just opens browser directly
4. Existing tests updated to remove client_secret references

## Prerequisites
- Atlassian app must be reconfigured as a public client (no secret) at developer.atlassian.com
- User will provide the new client_id after reconfiguring

## Done when
- `jira auth login` opens browser with no prompts
- Token exchange works without client_secret
- Refresh works without client_secret
- All tests pass
- `uv run ruff check src/ tests/` passes

snapshot.title: Hardcode OAuth credentials with user override support
snapshot.body:
Hardcode client_id and client_secret as defaults in auth.py, but allow users to override via env vars or CLI flags. This follows the gh/gcloud pattern while giving security-conscious users the option to use their own app.

## Resolution order for credentials

1. CLI flags: `jira auth login --client-id X --client-secret Y`
2. Env vars: `JIRA_CLIENT_ID`, `JIRA_CLIENT_SECRET`
3. Hardcoded defaults in auth.py

## What needs to change

1. Add CLIENT_ID and CLIENT_SECRET constants in auth.py
2. login() uses defaults but accepts overrides
3. Remove interactive prompts from cli.py — use flags/env/defaults instead
4. Add --client-id and --client-secret flags to `auth login` subparser
5. Save whatever client_id/secret was used in instance config (for refresh to work)
6. from_config() reads client_id/secret from saved config (not hardcoded — the saved values might be user-overridden)
7. Update tests

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

1. login() uses hardcoded defaults when no overrides (tracer bullet)
2. CLI flags override defaults
3. Env vars override defaults (when no flags)
4. Saved config preserves whichever credentials were used
5. from_config() reads saved credentials for refresh

## Done when
- `jira auth login` works with no prompts (uses hardcoded defaults)
- `jira auth login --client-id X --client-secret Y` uses custom credentials
- `JIRA_CLIENT_ID=X JIRA_CLIENT_SECRET=Y jira auth login` uses env vars
- All tests pass

## Files
- src/jira/auth.py (add constants, credential resolution)
- src/jira/cli.py (replace prompts with flags, wire env vars)
- src/jira/client.py (from_config reads saved credentials)
- tests/test_auth.py (update)

snapshot.title: Auto-discover projects in fields sync
snapshot.body:
When running `jira fields sync` without --project, automatically discover all accessible projects and sync their type schemas. Currently it only syncs the field list and skips project schemas.

## What needs to change

1. In schema.sync(), when no project is specified, fetch all projects from the Jira API
2. Use GET /rest/api/3/project to list accessible projects
3. Sync type schemas for each discovered project
4. --project still works as a filter for syncing a single project

## Behaviors to cover (one vertical slice each)

1. sync() without project fetches project list and syncs all (tracer bullet)
2. sync() with --project still syncs only that project
3. Test with mocked project list endpoint

## Done when
- `jira fields sync` discovers and syncs all projects
- `jira fields sync --project DEV` still works
- All tests pass

## Files
- src/jira/schema.py (replace TODO with project discovery)
- tests/test_schema.py (add test for auto-discovery)

snapshot.title: Fix inspect-5p findings from full codebase review
snapshot.body:
Fix the high and medium findings from the 5-pass inspection.

## Findings to fix

### High
4. schema.py: KEY_TYPES includes 'any' — overly broad. Match on field ID (parent) instead of type.

### Medium
1. auth.py: wait_for_callback has no timeout — blocks forever if callback never arrives.
2. auth.py: save_login_config writes config with no file permissions restriction. Add chmod 600.
3. config.py: instance matching via substring is too loose. Match subdomain prefix only.
5. client.py: delete() bypasses Client.request() — add graceful empty-body handling.
6. cli.py: --set without = raises unhelpful ValueError. Add validation with clear error.
8. cli.py: cli() dispatch is 100+ lines. Extract handler functions.
10. test_client.py: delete test doesn't verify the no-JSON-parse behavior.
12. auth.py: import order within Python section not sorted.
13. cli.py: DEFAULT_CLIENT_ID/SECRET referenced directly in body — should flow through signature.

## Done when
- All findings fixed
- `uv run pytest` passes
- `uv run ruff check src/ tests/` passes

snapshot.title: Auto-convert plain text description to ADF
snapshot.body:
When a user passes a plain string for the description field (e.g. "description": "Fix the bug"), resolve_fields should auto-wrap it in the minimal ADF structure (doc > paragraph > text). Currently, plain strings pass through and Jira API v3 rejects them with a 400 error. Only wrap strings — dicts (already ADF) pass through untouched as they do today.

Done when:
- resolve_fields wraps string description values into ADF paragraph
- Dict description values pass through unchanged
- Tests cover both cases

snapshot.title: Auto-convert plain text description to ADF
snapshot.body:
When a user passes a plain string for the description field (e.g. "description": "Fix the bug"), resolve_fields should auto-wrap it in the minimal ADF structure (doc > paragraph > text). Currently, plain strings pass through and Jira API v3 rejects them with a 400 error. Only wrap strings — dicts (already ADF) pass through untouched as they do today.

Done when:
- resolve_fields wraps string description values into ADF paragraph
- Dict description values pass through unchanged
- Tests cover both cases

snapshot.title: Markdown to ADF conversion
snapshot.body:
Convert standard Markdown text to Atlassian Document Format (ADF) so agents can write descriptions in Markdown and have them auto-converted for the Jira API.

Uses mistune as the Markdown parser with a custom renderer that emits ADF nodes.

Progressive implementation:
1. Add mistune dependency
2. Block nodes: paragraphs, headings, code blocks, blockquotes, horizontal rules
3. Inline marks: bold, italic, inline code, strikethrough
4. Links
5. Lists: bullet and ordered (including nested)
6. Wire into resolve_fields — detect Markdown strings and convert
7. Update skill docs

Done when: Markdown strings in description/environment fields are auto-converted to proper ADF with all common Markdown elements supported.

snapshot.title: Add mistune dependency
snapshot.body:
Add mistune to project dependencies in pyproject.toml and verify it installs cleanly with uv sync.

Done when: uv sync succeeds and 'import mistune' works.

snapshot.title: Markdown to ADF: block nodes
Markdown to ADF: inline marks
snapshot.body:
Extend the ADF renderer to handle inline marks:
- **bold** → strong mark
- *italic* → em mark
- `code` → code mark
- ~~strikethrough~~ → strike mark

Marks are applied to text nodes as an array. Multiple marks can combine (e.g. bold+italic).

Done when: inline marks convert correctly, including nested combinations, with tests.

snapshot.title: Markdown to ADF: links
snapshot.body:
Extend the ADF renderer to handle links:
- [text](url) → text node with link mark {type: link, attrs: {href: url}}

Links can combine with other marks (bold link, etc).

Done when: links convert correctly with tests.

Wire markdown_to_adf into resolve_fields
snapshot.body:
Replace text_to_adf with markdown_to_adf in resolve_fields for ADF_FIELDS. When a string value is passed for description/environment, parse it as Markdown and convert to ADF.

This replaces the plain text_to_adf wrapper — Markdown is a superset of plain text, so plain strings still work (they become a single paragraph).

Update existing tests to use the new function. Remove text_to_adf if no longer needed.

Done when: resolve_fields uses markdown_to_adf, all existing tests pass, plain strings still work.

snapshot.title: add_comment should convert markdown to ADF
snapshot.body: jira issue comment sends the body as plain text in {"body": body}, but Jira API v3 expects ADF for comment bodies. The description field already converts markdown to ADF via adf.py — apply the same conversion to comments. The agent hit this when trying to comment on DEV-123 with markdown content and got a crash at client.py:50 add_comment.

snapshot.title: PUT/edit should handle 204 No Content response
snapshot.body: jira issue edit uses client.put() which calls response.json() unconditionally. The Jira API returns 204 No Content on successful PUT, causing json.JSONDecodeError on empty body. The agent hit this when editing DEV-123 with --raw-payload — the edit succeeded but jira-genie crashed parsing the response. Fix: check response status/content-length before calling .json().

snapshot.title: Support --file or stdin for long content in comment and edit
snapshot.body: Passing multiline markdown through bash --json or positional args is fragile — shell escaping of nested quotes, backticks, and unicode characters causes failures. The agent tried 4 different approaches (inline quotes, heredoc to file, simplified text, raw-payload) before succeeding. Add --file support for description/comment body, e.g.: jira issue comment DEV-123 --file /tmp/comment.md or echo '...' | jira issue comment DEV-123 --stdin. This avoids shell escaping entirely.

snapshot.title: Skill doc: add explicit update description pattern
snapshot.body: When the user said 'update the ticket', the agent reached for jira issue comment instead of jira issue edit --json '{"description": "..."}'. The skill doc lists comment under Workflow Operations but has no clear pattern for updating a ticket's description. Add a 'Common Agent Patterns' entry showing how to update description, and consider reordering so edit comes before comment in the doc.

snapshot.title: Formatted output should render ADF description as readable text
snapshot.body: jira issue get DEV-123 (formatted) returns only key, summary, status, assignee, priority, type — no description. jira issue get --raw includes description but as raw ADF (deeply nested JSON), forcing the agent to write inline Python to extract readable text. format_issue() should render ADF back to markdown/plaintext and include description, comments, and other rich fields in the formatted output. Agents shouldn't need --raw + custom code just to read a ticket.

snapshot.body: Update README, docs, and skill to use 'Jira Genie' as the product name instead of 'Jira CLI'. The CLI command stays as 'jira' (short, ergonomic). snapshot.title: Structured JSON error output instead of raw tracebacks
snapshot.body: When jira-genie crashes, agents see raw Python stacktraces and have to guess what went wrong. Wrap CLI errors in structured JSON: {"error": "message", "code": "type"} to stderr. Agents can parse failures and recover intelligently instead of retrying blindly.

snapshot.title: jira skill status command
snapshot.body: Add jira skill status to show where the skill is installed and which version. Checks each target directory (pi, claude, codex, agents) and reports installed/not-installed/outdated. Useful after install to verify things worked.

snapshot.title: Add --description shorthand to issue edit
snapshot.body: jira issue create has --summary as a shorthand. jira issue edit should have --description for the same reason — avoids wrapping a single field in --json. Usage: jira issue edit DEV-123 --description 'Updated problem statement'.

snapshot.title: bulk edit should support --json
snapshot.body: jira bulk edit only supports --set key=value flags. Add --json support like issue edit has, so agents can pass structured field updates: jira bulk edit DEV-1 DEV-2 --json '{"parent": "DEV-100", "priority": "P1: High"}'.

snapshot.title: Split _handle_issue into per-subcommand handlers
snapshot.body: cli.py _handle_issue is ~80 lines of elif branches for get/edit/create/transition/assign/comment/link. Each branch is independent. Split into handle_issue_get, handle_issue_edit, etc. dispatched from a dict. Same pattern as the top-level command dispatch.

snapshot.title: Verify jira completion install output after package rename
snapshot.body: jira completion install prints shell setup instructions. Verify the output is correct after the package rename from jira to jira_genie. The register-python-argcomplete command should still reference the jira CLI name, not the package name.

snapshot.title: Extract safe_request helper in client.py until requestspro handles 204
snapshot.body: 5 methods in IssueSubClient manually do response = self.session.request(...); response.raise_for_status(); return response.json() if response.content else None. This works around requestspro's Client.request always calling response.json(). Extract a local helper to deduplicate until the upstream fix lands. Once requestspro is fixed, delete the helper and go back to self.put/self.post.
